title: Observed project evidence is separate from declared module architecture
summary: The headless C++ project index reports deterministic filesystem, include, compiler-profile, component, and build-target observations without treating inferred ownership or dependencies as an authoritative synthetic module design.
details: A paired header/source stem is an inferred physical component; it is not automatically a user-approved project module.
CMake target membership and downstream target consumption are recorded separately because a target may aggregate several logical modules and a header may be consumed by targets that do not own it.

Direct spelled includes produce evidence-backed component edges. Their interface/implementation origin, conditional preprocessor context, provider resolution route, and source line remain inspectable.
The future module manifest may accept, split, merge, or override inferred components and edges. Generated indexes remain rebuildable views rather than competing sources of truth.
The index process is read-only. It never configures CMake, creates a File API query, edits a project, or claims that an absent compile database/codemodel is an error in the inspected project.
rationale: The owner wants a rigid Lakos-inspired physical module DAG, but only after a substantial inspection and idea phase grounded in real personal projects.
Filesystem layout, include spelling, and build targets are strong evidence but each can disagree with intended ownership; silently promoting heuristics would make later code generation dangerous.
Keeping observation and declaration separate lets the first index support include intelligence and header synchronization before the module schema is settled.
alternatives: Treat each directory as a module; rejected because directories may contain several cohesive units or only organizational groupings.
Treat each CMake target as a module; rejected because dans-vk2 currently aggregates all first-party implementation files into one executable target.
Write an inferred manifest during scanning; rejected because ambiguous architecture requires an explicit later decision and preview.

provenance.note: Owner approved a Lakos-inspired, codegen-heavy synthetic module system after a preparatory project audit and accepted the proposed staged ordering.
Paired components and include strata exist while first-party CMake target granularity remains a single executable, demonstrating why these concepts must be separate.

evidence.claim: The index separately pins target ownership, consumer targets, compilation profiles, direct include edges, conditional provenance, vendored providers, and cycles.
Scanning emits JSON only and contains no project mutation or CMake configuration path.

questions: Does the project index decide which files form a synthetic module?
Is a CMake target treated as one project module?
Can scanning silently write an inferred module manifest?

last_verified.method: Owner-approved architecture followed by deterministic fixture and real-project read-only scans.
